meta.description: Personal health and training database — Todd Blackhurst AI coaching system

profile.name: Todd Blackhurst
conditions: migraine (chronic, managed with Topiramate prophylaxis + Imitrex acute)
goals: reach 159 lbs at 13% BF by August 1, 2026
gain 2 lbs lean mass (trunk priority — currently rated LOW)

oura_biology_baselines.notes: Oura Biology personal baselines. VO2 Max 38.9 (Below Average — cardio fitness improvement goal). HRV baseline 32.5 ms (any reading below this signals recovery deficit). Avg sleep 6h 25m/night — chronic mild sleep debt.

coaching_flags: Topiramate may reduce heat tolerance — monitor during warm-weather or high-HR sessions
Pitavastatin: watch for disproportionate muscle soreness/fatigue not explained by training load
Berberine before lunch and dinner — avoid scheduling training immediately post-dose to prevent glucose dip
Morning strength sessions avoid Berberine conflict entirely
Magnesium glycinate supports sleep — do not adjust or question timing
Migraine day = mandatory rest or significant session downgrade — no exceptions

nutrition_targets.notes: Targets set 2026-04-11. ~380 kcal/day deficit (between mild and moderate). Protein elevated for anabolic resistance at age 57. Fat raised for hormone support. Recalculate if weight changes ±3 kg or training frequency shifts.

body_composition.notes: Baseline from Bevel 30-day Progress Report (Nov 5 – Nov 24)
End-point from Bevel 30-day Progress Report — marked as Low on fat distribution
Bevel weekly scan (week of Apr 6). Visceral Fat Level 2.0. BF% 16.2%, weight 75.9 kg / 167.3 lbs. Primary VAT reduction target ongoing.

Hume Body Pod full scan, updated Apr 10. Air-displacement plethysmography (higher BF% than BIA/Bevel). Trunk muscle Low — core/anti-rotation priority confirmed. Bilateral arm fat High. Goal weight 165 lbs.

Bevel morning scan Apr 11, 4:29 AM. Weight stable at 166.0 lbs. Body fat 17.4% — excellent control. Lean mass 137.1 lbs (78.1% muscle) — preserved well. Visceral fat Lv 3.0 indicates modest VAT. BMR 1779 kcal baseline for deficit calculations.

Bevel morning scan Apr 12, 6:47 AM. Weight 165.8 lbs — down 0.2 lbs from Apr 11. BF 16.3% stable. Lean mass 138.8 lbs (79.1%). Visceral Fat Lv 2.0 (down from 3.0 on Apr 11 — positive signal). BMR 1779 kcal consistent. Trending toward 165 lb goal.

recovery_sleep.notes: Low strain day — Oura. Productive Sleep Debt Recovery noted. Outdoor cycling session same morning.
Sleep Quality 55%, below average. Time in Bed 7h 43m, Asleep 7h 17m. Wind-down 9:20 PM, target bedtime 9:50 PM.
Recovery 58% Normal. Sleep 73% Above normal. Deep Sleep 59m (notable). HR Dip 14%. Sleep Bank -1h 49m. Cardio Load 29 Detraining. Sleep HRV avg 31ms / max 126ms. Lowest HR 56 bpm.
Significant HRV Drop — Recovery crashed to 26% (HRV 19.3 ms, ~20ms below baseline). Despite 1h 6min deep sleep. Sleep started Apr 3 at 10:19 PM. Reduction in training intensity indicated.
Recovery Breakthrough — HRV surged to 51.4 ms (+52% rebound from Apr 4's 26%). Nervous system adapting well to training. Sleep 5h 50m (started Apr 4 at 10:56 PM). Sleep Score 69 FAIR.

Recovery Slide 41% — HRV 36.2 ms (Recovery metric), 25 ms avg overnight. HRV/HR spike after Apr 8 high-strain session. Sleep Score 69 FAIR. 5h 28m asleep / 7h 4m in bed (9:21 PM – 4:25 AM). Efficiency 77%. Deep 38m, REM 65m. HR Dip 15%. Debt -2h 13m.

Resilience Signal — steady 57% recovery despite Apr 10 high-strain session. HR dropped 3.1 bpm. Rise: 6h 48m sleep / 8h 13m in bed (9:18 PM – 5:31 AM), Efficiency 83%, Deep 43m, REM 1h 18m, Latency 22m, Restfulness Good, Timing Optimal, Debt -2h 30m Moderate. AutoSleep: 7h 14m total, REM 2h 6m, Deep 30m, Debt -2h 8m, Sleep Time 9:38 PM. HR Dip 14%. Lowest HR 58. HRV avg 26ms / max 151ms. Avg HR 64. SpO2 95.2%.

Recovery Breakthrough — Oura 68% (Above normal, +12% from yesterday). Rise sleep score 70/GOOD. REM above normal at 2h 28m (Oura) / 1h 2m (Rise). HRV 41.7ms above personal baseline of 32.5ms. Sleep short at 6h 15m–6h 54m. Moderate sleep debt (~2h 12m). Latency 37m — attributed to elevated metabolism from prior workout. Restfulness flagged. Respiratory rate slightly below normal (14.1 rpm) — monitor. SpO2 96.1% normal. Training guidance: Amber-Green, full session OK, HR <122 bpm. | Motra supplement: HRV Stabilizing (7d +1.6ms, 30d -3.8ms), RHR 62.7 bpm Stable, VO2 Max 39.6 Good (+4.9 over 90d), Weight 166.3 lbs (30d high, +0.8 lbs/wk trend). Training status: Amber-Green confirmed.

Positives: deep sleep 59min/18% solid, restfulness Optimal, HR stable. Fasted morning walk already done (5:16 AM, 2.43mi, Zone 0-1, 16% Activity Strain). Training guidance: Amber — proceed with programmed strength session, HR cap 122 bpm, treat first sets as warm-in, reduce Block C if fatigue surfaces.

Total active calories today: 371 (walk 187 + cycle 184). Double cardio on active recovery day — volume appropriate given Easy effort on both sessions.

Active calories 143, total 191. Pattern: second consecutive day at Tanzi District Indoor Cycle (same session Apr 14: 31:10, avg HR 100, Easy). Consistent execution.

sprint_protocol.rationale: HGH stimulus via short maximal efforts. At 57, sprint intervals drive growth hormone more effectively than steady-state cardio. Supports VAT reduction, muscle preservation, and metabolic adaptation.
sprint_protocol.equipment: Matrix upright stationary bike — Floor 1, WorldGym Taichung
sprint_protocol.placement: Saturday standalone session — NOT on designated recovery days (Tue/Thu), NOT as a strength-day finisher

skip_if: Friday session ran > 80 min or felt heavy

protocol.warm_up_notes: Easy pedaling, conversational pace, build HR to 85-100 bpm
protocol.cool_down_notes: Easy pedaling until HR returns below 110 bpm
protocol.hr_note: Sprint sessions intentionally exceed 122 bpm cap — this is therapeutic, not a compliance failure. HR cap only applies to Z1/Z2 recovery days.

weeks_1_2.notes: 4 x 20 sec all-out, 3 min easy pedaling recovery between efforts

weeks_3_4.notes: Add 1 rep only — maintain 3 min rest if recovery data marginal

weeks_5_plus.notes: Conditional — only progress if HRV trend supports it and gate conditions routinely met

upcoming_session.why: Week 2 Wednesday pulls from a different angle than Week 1 Monday. Front Lat Pulldown (neutral grip) replaces the wide-grip Hammer Strength — different ROM and lat emphasis. Meadows Row added for athletic, anti-rotation row stimulus (left first — right-side corrective). Hip Thrust stays as the anchor for posterior chain progression tracking. Cable Pull-Through swaps in for Low Step-Up — keeps the hinge pattern, removes the step-up load on hip OA. Face Pull, Suitcase Carry, and Dead Bug carry over as structural correction anchors. Open Book Stretch in prep adds thoracic rotation warm-up ahead of the pulling work.

exercises.name: Open Book Stretch
exercises.cues: Left side first. Lie on your side, knees stacked at 90°. Top arm sweeps across to the floor behind you — follow it with your eyes, not just your arm. The rotation should come from your thoracic spine, not your lower back. Hold the end range 2 sec before returning. If right side rotates less, add one extra rep.

exercises.name: Banded Hip Abduction
exercises.cues: Stand sideways to anchor or use a loop band just above knees. Controlled push outward — no hip hike. Left side first. This is glute med activation for the hinge work ahead, not a fatigue set.

exercises.name: Hanging Scapular Pull
exercises.cues: Dead hang from the pull-up bar. Without bending the elbows, depress and retract the shoulder blades — like trying to put them in your back pockets. Hold 1 sec at the top of the pull. This sets the scapular position for all the pulling work. Quality over speed.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Apart
exercises.cues: Arms straight, band at chest height. Pull apart to a T — squeeze the rear delts at end range for 1 sec. Don't let the hands drift downward. Slow and deliberate.

exercises.name: Machine Front Lat Pulldown
exercises.load: Match comfortable Week 1 lat pulldown weight · test +5 kg set 3 if all reps clean
exercises.cues: Neutral or underhand grip — different angle from the wide-grip Hammer Strength you ran Week 1 Monday. Initiate with shoulder blades down before arms bend. Pull elbows toward hip pockets. Full hang at the top — don't cut the range. 2 sec hold at the bottom of each rep.

exercises.name: Meadows Row
exercises.cues: Left side first every set. Stand perpendicular to the landmine, stagger stance, hinge forward. Pull the bar to your hip — elbow drives back and up, not out. Anti-rotation demand is high: your torso should stay square. Squeeze the lat and rhomboid at the top 1 sec. If you're rotating into the pull, drop the load.

exercises.name: Machine Hip Thrust
exercises.load: Match or add 5 kg vs Week 1 Monday — anchor for progression
exercises.cues: Track load vs Week 1 Monday. Full hip extension and glute squeeze at top — hold 1 sec. Don't hyperextend the lower back. Feet hip-width, toes slightly out. If hip pinching appears at any point, reduce range immediately.

exercises.name: Cable Pull-Through
exercises.load: Moderate — hip hinge, not low-back grind
exercises.cues: Cable between legs from a low pulley. Hinge at hips — soft knee, neutral spine, sit back like you’re loading a deadlift. Drive hips forward to stand, squeezing glutes at the top. The cable should stay close to your body throughout. Hip OA: this is a long-lever glute/hamstring pattern — safe and effective. Drop weight before form breaks.

exercises.name: Cable Face Pull
exercises.load: Match or add 2.5 kg vs last week — shoulder corrective anchor
exercises.cues: Rope at eye level, neutral grip. Pull to forehead — elbows high and wide at the end, not low. External rotation is the point, not the pull. 2 sec hold at end range. If right arm drives forward on the return, slow that side down intentionally.

exercises.name: KB Suitcase Carry
exercises.cues: Left hand only. Walk tall — neutral spine, ribs down, no lateral lean into the weight. Resist the pull through your obliques, not by hiking the hip. Slow controlled stride. This is your right-side dominance corrective — the challenge is standing symmetrically against asymmetric load.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.cues: Low back pinned to floor throughout — shorten range before grinding. Left side first. Breathe out on extension, breathe in on return. 3 sec down, 1 sec hold at full extension. If the low back loses the floor, you’ve gone too far. This is anti-rotation and core endurance — not a conditioning set.

upcoming_session.coach_note: Week 2 Wednesday Pull + Posterior Chain. Pulling at a different angle than Week 1 Monday — Front Lat Pulldown (neutral grip) vs wide-grip Hammer Strength. Meadows Row added for athletic anti-rotation pulling stimulus — left side first, right-side dominance corrective. Hip Thrust is your progression anchor vs Week 1 Monday — track the load. Cable Pull-Through replaces Low Step-Up: same posterior chain work, less hip OA demand. Face Pull and Suitcase Carry carry over as structural correction standards. Time cap 65 min — drop Dead Bug first if Block C runs long.

coach_questions.text: Where do you actually feel your right-side dominance most — in the gym, or in daily life?
coach_questions.context: Carrying bags, reaching, stairs, sitting crooked? The real-life signal often reveals more than any single exercise. This calibrates which correctives matter most to prioritize.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. right shoulder always rides higher when I carry groceries, or I notice my right hip loads more on stairs…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: calibrate structural correction priority in daily vs. gym context

coach_questions.text: When during the day does your energy actually peak — and is that when you typically train?
coach_questions.context: Misalignment between peak energy and training time is one of the most underrated performance drains. This shapes when Coach should front-load nutrition and how to read readiness scores.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. I'm sharpest 8-10am but often train at noon because of schedule…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: align training timing recommendations with biological energy peak

coach_questions.text: On a heavy preaching/ministry week — how does it actually affect your training energy?
coach_questions.context: Cognitive and emotional load from ministry is real physiological stress. Coach needs to know whether heavy ministry weeks = lower readiness, or whether training is your release valve.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. big sermon week = mentally drained but still want to train / or = body feels flat…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: model ministry load as a readiness variable alongside HRV and sleep

coach_questions.text: On cardio days — what type actually feels good vs. what feels like punishment?
coach_questions.context: Coach knows you train cardio on off-days, but not what format works best mentally and physically given hip OA. This directly affects what Coach recommends for cardio options.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. bike is fine, treadmill walking is boring, swimming is great but inconvenient…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: build an accurate cardio preference profile for off-day programming

coach_questions.text: What's the specific feeling — physical or mental — that tells you a session was actually worth it?
coach_questions.context: Not 'I worked hard.' The precise signal. Pump? Movement quality? A specific exercise that landed? Coach uses this to calibrate when to call a session a win vs. when to adjust.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. when I leave feeling taller / when I hit all my pull-up reps / when the last set felt controlled…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: define personal session success signal for post-session coaching accuracy

coach_questions.text: What does your hydration look like on training days — before, during, and after?
coach_questions.context: Dehydration tanks HRV and perceived effort faster than most people realize. Coach has no visibility into this. Even a rough pattern is useful.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. coffee in the morning, water during session, forget to drink after…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: assess hydration as a hidden factor in readiness and HR control

coach_questions.text: When you travel for ministry — what training do you actually manage to do?
coach_questions.context: Travel breaks are predictable stress on your program. Knowing what's realistic on the road lets Coach build a proper travel protocol instead of a generic 'do what you can.'
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. hotel gym basics, bodyweight only, usually nothing for 3+ days…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: build a realistic travel training protocol based on actual behavior

coach_questions.text: Outside of hip OA — any other past injuries or surgeries Coach should factor into exercise selection?
coach_questions.context: Things that healed years ago can still create compensation patterns today. Shoulder, knee, back, wrist — even old stuff matters for long-term programming.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. right knee had surgery in 2010, left shoulder impingement a few years ago…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: build complete injury history to prevent accidental re-aggravation

coach_questions.text: What's the last thing you typically eat or drink before bed — and does it vary much night to night?
coach_questions.context: Late-night eating (especially sweets vs. protein vs. nothing) directly impacts Oura sleep scores and overnight HRV. Coach can't see this from the data alone.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. usually nothing after 8pm / or I always have a small snack around 9…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: connect late-eating patterns to overnight HRV and sleep quality trends

coach_questions.text: Which floor of WorldGym do you prefer to start on — and does the starting floor affect your mindset?
coach_questions.context: Coach has the layout rules, but not your personal preference. If starting on Floor 3 puts you in the right headspace, that's worth baking into the default session structure.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. I like starting on F2 and going up / or F3 activation feel is better for me mentally…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: personalize gym floor sequencing to match preference vs. just logical flow

coach_questions.text: What do your off-days actually look like — intentional rest, light activity, or just whatever happens?
coach_questions.context: Recovery days aren't all equal. Walking, stretching, and light movement all affect the readiness picture. Coach needs to know what 'rest day' actually means for you.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. usually a lot of walking, or mostly sedentary with desk work, or I try to do light stretching…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: model off-day activity level when interpreting mid-week readiness data

coach_questions.text: Do you train with music — and does the type of music change how hard you actually push?
coach_questions.context: Sounds minor but isn't. Tempo and arousal from music is a real variable in effort output. Some athletes train completely differently with headphones on vs. off.
coach_questions.placeholder: e.g. always headphones — worship keeps me focused / or I need high BPM to push hard…
coach_questions.coaching_purpose: understand arousal inputs that affect session performance
